Author: Vitalii Zdanovskyi
Title: Is AI taking over your coding job? Not necessarily.
Text:
Is AI taking over your coding job? Not necessarily.
It seems that now, an AI hype has reached its peak level. Everyone is excited, frustrated, and afraid at the same time. Some people are psyched by the near-limitless possibilities an AI would bring. Others are skeptical and are channeling a lot of hate towards the first group. Some people are pulling the copyright card and stating that an AI work is just a mimic of theirs and is not original. In reality, there are two types of people: those who aim to replace everyone with AI, and those who fight against it, as they’re afraid to lose their jobs. Who gets it right? No one! I’m pretty sure both groups are wrong. I’ve been exploring AI and trying to figure out where I stand. While I’m still not sure how I feel about AI, more and more, I come to the conclusion that the right answer is this: we need to learn to co-exist with AI in our work. For starters, here’s some revelation to all the tech people:
AI can replace coders, not engineers.
The CEO of Anthropic, Dario Amodei, had made a statement recently that AI will write 90% of the code in 3 to 6 months. And while I’m sure that such a short term is optimistic to say the least, it’s still hard to deny that we’re going in that direction. But if this claim makes you panic, it’s time to take a good look at what you’re doing work-wise. Do keep in mind that coding and building/designing big and complex ecosystems are not the same things. And we’re far away from AI being able to do the latter.
I’m not afraid of being replaced because I know that I bring more to the table than just a bunch of code. The way I see it, finding business problems and transforming them into tech solutions is a crucial part of any software development job. It’s harder and way more important than knowing how to code. And as of today, I’m not aware of an AI, that is able to do that. If that’s not the case for you, then yes, you might be in trouble. But I’m sure, most people are better than that 😉
It can be a partnership, not a war.
As for the AI-engineer relationships, I think most engineers could eventually benefit from it. While originally being skeptical about the idea, I used an AI to implement an entire feature on my pet project and was quite impressed with the result. An AI was able to understand my tech stack and figure out what my project was about. From there on, all I had to do was to say what I needed it to do (using a normal human language) and watch the magic happen. A bit of context here: I wanted my ReactJS web-app to be able to work offline. It was a “nice to have” kind of feature, so not really a main priority. Plus, I didn’t have much time to do it myself, so I decided to try an AI just for the fun of it, and was truly impressed. So now I can see a future where engineers will treat an AI like a junior developer. AI will be doing the implementation under an engineer’s supervision. The engineer would provide instructions, guidance, and make corrections, so that AI’s code is in line with the general system. I feel like it’s already possible to some extent, with the current state of AI. But I’m just not ready to trust it that much. For me, it’s like having a brain surgery, performed by an AI-operated robot, with a brain surgeon standing right next to it. I’d probably go for it if it was something simpler, like an appendectomy, but not for this. But that’s just my personal experience with it. Regardless of what I think, an AI is rapidly growing into a force to be reckoned with. So even if it will not take over your job, there’s a good chance it can help you with it. So knowing how and when to use an AI would definitely give you an edge in both business and job search. Whether it’s performing a boring routine task or building an entire new product under your guidance, an AI can be a great partner and definitely shouldn’t be your enemy.
But they say AI can do it all.
Again, I’m not as optimistic about the timeframe, yet someday, an AI will be able to design and build an entire half-decent system from scratch. Will it happen? For sure. Will it take over the entire industry? I highly doubt it. I’ve been doing software development for more than ten years now. Over this time, I survived quite a few “next big things” that were supposed to kill my industry. First, there were CMSes like Joomla/WordPress, then came website builders such as Squarespace, and platforms like Webflow. Then there was all the hype with no-code solutions like Notion or Airtable. Those are all great tools, and I use some of them myself on a daily basis. While all these tools allow you to build a website without coding, none of them have killed developer jobs (some even created more jobs for that matter). Now there’s AI. And yet again, people claim it’ll disrupt an industry. I’m sure there will be a lot of great website/software builders based on AI, and they’ll take their fair share of the market. But it doesn’t mean software development jobs will die. They may transform with an AI becoming a bigger part of the development process, but I’m sure that software dev jobs will be there. Worst-case scenario — someone still needs to create new AIs 😂
It’s the same thing as the self-driving cars hype. Everyone screams that all the cars are soon going to be self-driving. Yet all we have so far is self-driving cars taking a small chunk of the market, and the majority of car manufacturers are using limited self-driving capabilities to prevent accidents.
That small chunk may grow bigger with time, but I don’t think we’ll see the world with AI doing all the jobs and driving all the cars any time soon. Think of commercial airlines for a second. The majority of planes have had autopilot since the mid-50s, yet I don’t see too many pilots looking for new jobs 70 years later.
If there’s anything to take away, it’s this: regardless of your industry, things like expertise and real human experience are not really replaceable with an AI. So if you’re afraid that an AI might have a chance of replacing you, you should take a hard look at what kind of value you bring to the table. Chances are, there’s something that no AI will be able to do.
So what do we do?
While you most likely won’t get replaced, AI is changing the way we approach work. Whether we like it or not, the way we do things is going to change in the next few years. But if you’re able to keep up and adopt AI in your work process, you will only benefit from it. So while retaining a bit of skepticism, be curious and keep an open mind.
